Okay, welcome to section one. This is the pre-Headline qualifier, and it's all about getting your prospect to essentially do their best Robert De Niro taxi driver impression minus the whole rampage at the end thing. Only what it's about is getting their ears to perk up, to be unequivocally unambiguously clear that what follows is meant to be read by them. But not only do they need to be clear that it's for them, there needs to be an immediate reason for them to care. So what you're coaching in this element is immediate self-selection, this feeling of like, oh hey, that's me. Another thing you're coaching is exclusive empowerment, something that uniquely entitles them or qualifies them to read on. And this could be positive or negative, which you'll see as we get deeper into this module. And finally, you want to give them a feeling of synchronicity, almost like this, wow, perfect timing, this is finding me in the exact moment it needs to. And when you have those elements going, you really create that beginning of that slippery slope of them being hyper-engaged, hyper-aware that what follows is designed and meant to be read by them. So there are going to be five ways to do it. Version one is what I call the implied injustice. And the implied injustice goes like this. So the basic template of it is important question for specific avatar who still haven't or don't, and this is going to make more sense when you get into the example. Biggest problem or most important desirable outcome. Now what this allows you to do in a really short economical way is call out your prospect and immediately identify a way, embed the promise or outcome, and by using the word still imply that there's an injustice, a gap, not a could but a should, which is a really important principle to grasp. Aspiration has the energy of someday, it's less urgent. It's like so high up on Maslow's hierarchy of needs that you need a freaking oxygen mass to get there. And aspiration, unfortunately for marketers, is something that people only pursue once they have all their other stuff in order. And the thing is, nobody ever feels like they have all their stuff in order. So when you work on aspiration and aspiration alone, it encourages stuckness and inertia. But on the other hand, we are literally wired to invest energy and attention in fixing what's wrong, bringing balance back to what is currently imbalanced than we do in chasing something new. So there's more urgency in restoring equilibrium. So by placing the outcome as a should, something that based on who they are, what abilities they have, what they've already achieved, that they should be able to realize in their life, we create this chasm that invites more energy and resourcefulness to bridge it. And the funny thing is, we could be talking about the exact same outcome from both an aspirational perspective, both a could and a should. But what we're doing here is we're moving it lower on that hierarchy of needs. From a point of self-actualization, I'll do it once everything else is met, and we're bringing it down instead to a point of survival. So it's less of a mental and emotional climb to get there. And remember, this ties back into the fact that most people reading your sales message will be in a state of inertia. They'll have that backseat passenger, their lizard brain, who dampens any desire for immediate change or transformation unless it's completely and utterly necessary to restore bounds to their reality. So your first goal is to overcome that inertia, to create energy, invite resourcefulness, and create the container for coaching that transformation between point A and point B. A perceived disequilibrium triggers the body to move to homeostasis and recruits the energy and resources to do it. So it's not that we're contriving unnecessary panic or urgency, we're just leveraging our most basic wiring to help drive our clients to accomplish amazing things which they'd otherwise regulate to someday status. Cool, with all that said, let's see how this plays out in real time. So this is an example taken from Amy Porterfield's ListBuilder's Lab 2.0. And it goes like this, important question for coaches, experts, and course creators who still don't have a profitable email list of over 2,500 engaged, ready to be subscribers. From this one example, you can see that we're seeding the promise right from the beginning in the section that literally 100% of our readers will be reading. And we're doing it in the moment of least resistance because our offer hasn't been announced yet, there's no price tag yet assigned to that outcome, it's perfectly safe to have 2,500 subscribers as your goal. Now this may be enough to get things rolling, but if you really want to expand on the implied injustice, here is another way to do it. So version two would be important question for instant identifier, who've big accomplishment but still haven't been able to, big promise. Now this one's similar to the first, except we're adding an extra layer of what I like to call exclusive empowerment, where taking an accomplishment or trait or something they're proud of and using that to ramp up the sense of injustice or disequilibrium even further. So here's an example of what that looks like. Career defining question for yoga professionals who despite completing over 500 hours of teacher training still don't have a sustainable business. Now notice in this case that we're taking the 500 hours of teacher training, which is a point of pride, a massive accomplishment that by all measures should have been able to propel the prospect towards a sustainable business. And we're using that fact to create even more discord or disharmony. Now this isn't creating or conjuring pain out of thin air, this is simply inviting your prospect to get really honest and intimate with the pain that's already there. And the truth is most people don't parlay their gifts, advantages or accomplishments into the most favorable outcome. They fail to find the full potential. So this type of pre-heline qualifier hits them right between the eyes with that fact before guiding them towards remedying that injustice. Now a third option is what I like to call the struggle is real and it goes like this. Important question for instant identifier who are experiencing short version of moment of highest tension. Now this one gets straight to the point. If you're dealing with a problem that your prospect is already likely hyper aware of and more importantly motivated to solve, you don't need to necessarily focus on creating so much discord and injustice. Since they already identify so clearly with the pain, all you really have to do is call it out in a shortened version of the moment of the highest tension that we learned about in module two. So here's what that might look like. Important question for first time breastfeeding moms worried that their newborn isn't getting enough milk. Now this is a problem that is literally all consuming and literally keeping this prospect up at night. So you don't need to exacerbate it with that exclusive empowerment or other perceived injustices. You just need to join them in that existing conversation. Now if you feel for whatever reason like leading with pain or implied injustice isn't appropriate for your audience. These final two versions focus more on pleasure and desired outcome. But I do recommend you try to make the first three work before going here and only going here if you're certain your prospect is already committed to the positive outcome that your product enables. So version four is called keeping the dream alive and it goes like this. Important question for immediate identifier who are actively trying to achieve big promise. Now let's see how this plays out. So critical make or break question for ambitious impact driven female executives leaders and founders committed to scaling beyond one million in revenue in 2018. And when you look at this the goal is clear one million revenue in 2018. The identifier is clear female executives leaders and founders. And for this audience they already have enough awareness of this goal. They already have enough invested in this goal that it is enough to simply call it out. Now to take this one step further we have the graduate important question for immediate identifier who've already big accomplishment and want to achieve big promise by specific time frame. So this is the last one I'll give you. And just like how we used exclusive empowerment before this is using it in more of a positive sense so you could really do the same. It's about padding them on the back over previous accomplishment and tapping into their desire to parlay that into their next big win with your help. So here's what that one looks like. Season defining question for 15 handicap golfers looking to break 80 for the first time this spring. So even in this very short headline you know who it's for specifically it's 15 handicap golfers which at the same time is something they're proud of that's an achievement. I'm not really a golfer but I'm assuming that's a big achievement and looking to break 80 for the first time this spring is a very clear identifiable goal which is probably already present in their mind if they are a serious golfer at a 15 handicap. So those are the five different versions I invite you to play around with. Feel free to use all of them you know to test different types of headlines for your product and then when you're done meet me in the next section where we will elaborate on this with the question loop headline. I'll see you in the next lesson.
